summary: Gruber reacts to Bloomberg's report that Apple is developing its own LLM framework called 'Ajax' and an internal chatbot called 'Apple GPT.' He argues the news itself is unsurprising — it would be shocking if Apple weren't working on this — and instead focuses his critique on Bloomberg's incentive structure, where reporters receive bonuses tied to market-moving news. He traces the immediate stock movements (Apple up 2.3%, Microsoft down) to the exact minute Gurman's report hit Bloomberg Terminals, noting the spike quickly reversed as people realized there was no real news. He frames Bloomberg's reporting style as fundamentally shaped by selling Terminal subscriptions to day traders.
key_points: Apple developing its own LLM framework (Ajax) and chatbot (Apple GPT) is entirely expected and not genuinely newsworthy
Bloomberg reporters are financially incentivized to publish market-moving news, which creates a structural bias toward sensationalism
The stock movements — Apple's brief 2.7% jump and Microsoft's drop at exactly 12:04pm — demonstrate Bloomberg Terminal's role as a tool for day traders getting early access to reports
Xcode's lack of AI code generation features is a notable gap compared to competitors like VS Code
The 'no moat' theory suggests Apple building its own LLM undermines the long-term value of Microsoft's OpenAI partnership
money_quotes: It's not surprising that Apple has a project like this underway. It would be rather shocking if they didn't.
They're incentivized financially to make mountains out of molehills, and craters out of divots, to maximize the immediate effect of their reporting on stock prices.
No other news organization I'm aware of has an incentive system like this for reporters — but no other news organization has a business like the Bloomberg Terminal.

It then mostly came back down to where it was, presumably after everyone realized there really wasn't any news here other than the fact that Apple is working on something we all presumed they were working on.

Once you view Bloomberg's original reporting through this prism — that most of their original reporting is delivered with the goal of moving the stock prices of the companies they're reporting on, for the purpose of proving the value of a Bloomberg Terminal's hefty subscription fee to day-trading gamblers — a lot of their seemingly inexplicable stylistic quirks don't seem so inexplicable any more.
They just seem a little gross.
Bloomberg employees, of course, know such information before it's published, but I'm sure never do anything untoward with it.
key_insight: Bloomberg's reporting on Apple's unsurprising AI efforts reveals more about Bloomberg's own incentive structure — where reporters are rewarded for moving stock prices — than it does about Apple's technology strategy.
